author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
Why God Does Not Immediately Cure Pride Itself. The Secret and Insidious Growth of Pride. Preventing and Subsequent Grace.
title: Why God Does Not Immediately Cure Pride Itself. The Secret and Insidious Growth of Pride. Preventing and Subsequent Grace.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Chapter 35 [XXXI.]—Why God Does Not Immediately Cure Pride Itself.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: The Secret and Insidious Growth of Pride.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: But I would indeed so treat these topics, as to confess myself ignorant of God’s deeper counsel, why He does not at once heal the very principle of pride, which lies in wait for man’s heart even in deeds rightly done; and for the cure of which pious souls, with tears and strong crying, beseech Him that He would stretch forth His right hand and help their endeavours to overcome it, and somehow tread and crush it under foot.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now when a man has felt glad that he has even by some good work overcome pride, from the very joy he lifts up his head and says: “Behold, I live; why do you triumph?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Nay, I live because you triumph.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Premature, however, this forwardness of his to triumph over pride may perhaps be, as if it were now vanquished, whereas its last shadow is to be swallowed up, as I suppose, in that noontide which is promised in the scripture which says, “He shall bring forth thy righteousness as the light, and thy judgment as the noonday;” provided that be done which was written in the preceding verse: “Commit thy way unto the Lord; trust also in Him, and He shall bring it to pass,”—not, as some suppose, that they themselves bring it to pass.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now, when he said, “And He shall bring it to pass,” he evidently had none other in mind but those who say, We ourselves bring it to pass; that is to say, we ourselves justify our own selves.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: In this matter, no doubt, we do ourselves, too, work; but we are fellow-workers with Him who does the work, because His mercy anticipates us.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: He anticipates us, however, that we may be healed; but then He will also follow us, that being healed we may grow healthy and strong.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: He anticipates us that we may be called; He will follow us that we may be glorified.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: He anticipates us that we may lead godly lives; He will follow us that we may always live with Him, because without Him we can do nothing.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now the Scriptures refer to both these operations of grace.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: There is both this: “The God of my mercy shall anticipate me,” and again this: “Thy mercy shall follow me all the days of my life.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Let us therefore unveil to Him our life by confession, not praise it with a vindication.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For if it is not His way, but our own, beyond doubt it is not the right one.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Let us therefore reveal this by making our confession to Him; for however much we may endeavour to conceal it, it is not hid from Him.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: It is a good thing to confess unto the Lord.
